coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform constructed by grafting various response groups on carbon dots with bromine active site for biosensing and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Guang-Yue
Guo, Lan
Chen, Shuai
Liu, Na-Zhen
Yu, Yong-Liang
coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional sensing platform with the advantages of simplicity, convenience and efficiency is critical to the improvement of application in biosensing and bioimaging. However, the lack of easily modified scaffold materials seriously hinders its various responsive sensing development. To solve this issue, a novel kind of carbon dots (namely rCDs) with easily modified bromine substitution site was developed by one-step solvothermal treatment of citric acid, ethylenediamine and 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride. Benefiting from the maintained structure of naphthalimide structure with bromine substitution site to contribute responsive yellow-green fluorescence emission and the stable blue fluorescence emission from citric acid and ethylenediamine, dual emissive ratiometric sensors based on rCDs could be conveniently fabricated by avoiding the combination of two components with distinct emission wavelength together. As proof, three ratiometric fluorescent probes for sensing formaldehyde (rCDs-FA), HSO3 - (rCDs-HSO3 -) and pH (rCDs-pH) featuring high stability, selectivity and biocompatibility were simply prepared. Moreover, the as-obtained probes were successfully applied to monitor dynamic variations of formaldehyde, HSO3 - and pH changes in living cells. Taken together, the established multifunctional ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform could be simply and effectively regulated through changing the grafted response groups. This work would provide a universal method to design more biosensing or bioimaging platforms by adjusting response groups in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing
Cell imaging
Formaldehyde
HSO3 -
pH

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and characterization of rCDs-based multifunctional sensing platform
Ratiometric fluorescent sensing in aqueous solution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21922402 , 21874017 and 21727811 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( N2005027 ). Special thanks are due to the instrumental analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University.

This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922402, 21874017 and 21727811), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2005027). Special thanks are due to the instrumental analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and particle size distributions (inset) of (A) rCDs, (B) rCDs-FA, (C) rCDs-HSO3 - and (D) rCDs-pH. (E) FT-IR spectra and (F) full-scan XPS spectra of rCDs, rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - and rCDs-pH.

(A) Fluorescence spectra and photos (inset) of rCDs-FA in response to formaldehyde at various concentrations. (B) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I538/I459) of rCDs-FA in response to formaldehyde at various concentrations; Inset shows the linear relationship between I538/I459 and concentration of formaldehyde. (C) Fluorescence spectra and photos (inset) of rCDs-HSO3 - in response to HSO3 - at various concentrations. (D) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I534/I459) of rCDs- HSO3 - in response to HSO3 - at various concentrations; Inset shows the linear relationship between Log(I534/I459) and Log [HSO3 -]. (E) Fluorescence spectra and photos (inset) of rCDs-pH at different pH values. (F) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I524/I459) of rCDs-pH at different pH values; Inset shows the linear relationship between I459/I524 and pH values.

(A) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I538/I459) of the rCDs-FA before and after reacting with formaldehyde (FA) at different pH values. (B) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I534/I459) of the rCDs-HSO3 - before and after reacting with HSO3 - at different pH values. (C) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I538/I459) of the rCDs-FA upon the addition of various potential interfering species (1. Blank; 2. Ca2+; 3. Ba2+; 4. Zn2+; 5. Mg2+; 6. Ni2+; 7. Mn2+; 8. F-; 9. Br-; 10. I-; 11. NO2 -; 12. SO4 2-; 13. NO3 -; 14. Glycine; 15. Threonine; 16. Valine; 17. Methionine; 18. Proline; 19. Cysteine; 20. Histidine; 21. Serine; 22. Leucine; 23. Tryptophan; 24. Glutamic acid; 25. Glutathione; 26. Ascorbic acid; 27. H2O2, 28. Acetaldehyde; 29. Glutaraldehyde; 30. Mixture.). The concentration of glutaraldehyde is 500 μM, formaldehyde is 100 μM and other species are 1 mM. (D) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I534/I459) of the rCDs-HSO3 - upon the addition of various potential interfering species (1. Blank; 2. Ca2+; 3. Mg2+; 4. Cu2+; 5. Zn2+; 6. F-; 7. Br-; 8. I-; 9. NO3 -; 10. NO2 -; 11. CO3 2-; 12. SO4 2-; 13. Cysteine; 14. Glutathione; 15. Methionine; 16. Proline; 17. Threonine; 18. ClO-; 19. H2O2; 20. Formaldehyde; 21. Mixture.). The concentration of ClO- and H2O2 is 500 μM, and other species are 1 mM. (E) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I524/I459) of the rCDs-pH in PBS solutions at pH = 5 and 9 upon the addition of 1 mM various potential interfering species (1. Blank; 2. Ca2+; 3. Mg2+; 4. Cu2+; 5. Zn2+; 6. F-; 7. Br-; 8. I-; 9. NO3 -; 10. NO2 -; 11. HSO3 -; 12. Cysteine; 13. Glutathione; 14. Methionine; 15. Proline; 16. Threonine; 17. ClO-; 18. H2O2; 19. Formaldehyde; 20. Mixture.). (F) Photostability of rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - and rCDs-pH under continuous irradiation with a xenon lamp at 385 nm for 1 h.

(A) Fluorescence intensity ratio of rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - and rCDs-pH in the absence and presence of 2 M of NaCl. (B) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity ratio (I538/I459) of rCDs-FA treated with 5 and 15 μM formaldehyde. (C) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity ratio (I534/I459) of rCDs-HSO3 - treated with 100 μM NaHSO3. (D) Fluorescence reversibility of rCDs-pH against pH change between 5.0 and 9.0.

(A) Confocal laser scanning microscope (CLMS) images of rCDs-FA stained MCF-7 cells treated with 400 μM NaHSO3 or different concentration levels of FA (0, 100 µM, 500 µM). (B) CLMS images of rCDs-HSO3 - stained MCF-7 cells before and after treating with 1 mM NaHSO3. (C) CLMS images of rCDs-pH stained MCF-7 cells before and after treating with 50 μM chloroquine.
Construction of multifunctional sensing platform based on rCDs and its application in ratiometric fluorescent detection of formaldehyde (HCHO), HSO3 - and pH.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform constructed by grafting various response groups on carbon dots with bromine active site for biosensing and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Corrosion and Bio-fouling, Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Corrosion and Bio-fouling, Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Corrosion and Bio-fouling, Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Qingdao, 266071, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors have equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional sensing platform with the advantages of simplicity, convenience and efficiency is critical to the improvement of application in biosensing and bioimaging. However, the lack of easily modified scaffold materials seriously hinders its various responsive sensing development. To solve this issue, a novel kind of carbon dots (namely rCDs) with easily modified bromine substitution site was developed by one-step solvothermal treatment of citric acid, ethylenediamine and 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride. Benefiting from the maintained structure of naphthalimide structure with bromine substitution site to contribute responsive yellow-green fluorescence emission and the stable blue fluorescence emission from citric acid and ethylenediamine, dual emissive ratiometric sensors based on rCDs could be conveniently fabricated by avoiding the combination of two components with distinct emission wavelength together. As proof, three ratiometric fluorescent probes for sensing formaldehyde (rCDs-FA), HSO3 - (rCDs-HSO3 -) and pH (rCDs-pH) featuring high stability, selectivity and biocompatibility were simply prepared. Moreover, the as-obtained probes were successfully applied to monitor dynamic variations of formaldehyde, HSO3 - and pH changes in living cells. Taken together, the established multifunctional ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform could be simply and effectively regulated through changing the grafted response groups. This work would provide a universal method to design more biosensing or bioimaging platforms by adjusting response groups in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Multifunctional sensing platform for monitoring the levels of biological analytes with the advantages of simplicity, convenience and efficiency is of great importance for improving healthcare [1]. Through changing one recognition or reaction group of the multifunctional sensing platform, different sensors for various analytes can be easily fabricated. However, the lack of carrier scaffold materials with easily modified active site severely restricts the development of multifunctional sensing platform. Tan et al. reported aptamer-modified fluorescent-magnetic multifunctional nanoprobes based on magnetic γ-Fe2O3 and fluorescent CdSe/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) for sensing multiple pathogens [2]. It is demonstrated that magnetic nanoparticles and quantum dots used as the functional materials for sensing platforms are effective; however, they are suffered from complicated labeling process, limited range of detected objects and toxicity of QDs. Since the first intended synthesis in 2006, various carbon dots with excellent properties, such as robust photostability, favorable biocompatibility, excellent water solubility and simple synthesis, have been presented, which are promising to be a candidate of novel fluorescent scaffold [3–5]. However, due to the lack of an optically tunable group for binding recognition moieties, a great number of CDs can only achieve response by the interaction of simple surface groups such as carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups with the targets, which severely limits the types of detection targets. In addition, due to the non-specificity of the above functional groups, sensors developed by this type of CDs can only be combined with targets by H-bonding or surface-chelating effect, limiting their selectivity [6,7]. Despite there have been several reports dedicated to construct of probes based on CDs with specific recognition or reaction site to solve these problems [8,9], these methods can only introduce specific response group to detect specific target, i.e., they cannot be adjusted by changing response groups for different purposes. To the best of our knowledge, CDs with regulated active site to be easily modified by various specific response moieties for the construction of multifunctional sensing platform remain rare.

Imaging of bioactive species and microenvironment in living cells is of great importance to medical diagnostics as well as biological studies. For instance, formaldehyde, originating from histone demethylation or methylation of DNA, plays a critical role in the carbon cycle of metabolism [10,11]. Moreover, the raised formaldehyde in cells is possibly a critical factor in tissue cancerization and tumor progression [12]. Under physiological conditions, HSO3 - mainly generated from the metabolism of cysteine and glutathione [13]. Low concentration of HSO3 - contributes to vasodilation in cardiovascular systems, while the elevated HSO3 - is related to the diseases of stokes, migraine, lung cancer and brain cancer [14,15]. Intracellular pH plays an important role in cell function, signal transduction and apoptosis, and minute changes in pH can lead to significant changes in metabolism [16]. Determination of pH changes in living cells is of great significance in better understanding of pH-related physiological and pathological processes, as well as offering a good reference for diagnosis and treatment of several diseases [17]. Therefore, it is essential to develop effective probes for monitoring formaldehyde, HSO3 - and pH. So far, several techniques including chromatography, mass spectrum, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, electrochemical and fluorescence have been employed for the detection of formaldehyde, HSO3 - and pH [16,18,19]. Among various techniques, fluorescence sensor is noted for its sensitivity, simplicity and non-invasiveness [20–24]. But the commonly used single-signal fluorescent probes are easily affected by the effects of instrument, environment and probe concentrations. Ratiometric fluorescent sensing is considered as an effective strategy to improve detection accuracy, which is based on the ratio changes of the fluorescence intensities at various wavelengths [25–27]. Currently, a great number of ratiometric fluorescent probes based on CDs have been developed, which can be broadly divided into two categories. Probes of the first type are constructed by coupling organic fluorophore on the surface of CDs to provide a reference or response signal [28–30]. Although the properties of these probes are easy to be designed and regulated, their synthesis process is relatively tedious and inefficient. Probes of the second type are based on the different responses of two emission peaks of dual-wavelength emission CDs to the targets [31–34]. Nevertheless, these probes are difficult to be accurately designed and regulated, and lack versatility and scalability. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a simple, versatile and scalable CDs-based strategy for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of various analytes.

In this work, versatile and scalable CDs (namely rCDs) as novel scaffold material for construction of multifunctional ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform were synthesized by one-pot solvothermal treatment of citric acid, ethylenediamine and 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride ( Scheme 1). Benefiting from the maintained structure of naphthalimide structure with bromine substitution site to contribute responsive yellow-green fluorescence emission and the stable blue fluorescence emission from citric acid and ethylenediamine, dual emissive ratiometric sensors based on rCDs could be conveniently fabricated by avoiding the combination of two components with distinct emission wavelength together. On these bases, a series of ratiometric fluorescent probes can be designed and obtained by simple modifications of rCDs, i.e., functionalization of different recognition or reaction groups by the bromine substitution sites of rCDs. As proof, three ratiometric fluorescent probes for sensing formaldehyde (rCDs-FA), HSO3 - (rCDs-HSO3 -) and pH (rCDs-pH) featuring high photostability were synthesized by introducing hydrazine [35], aldehyde [36] and morpholine [37] as response groups. Moreover, intracellular ratiometric fluorescent imaging by these probes has been successfully demonstrated, and rCDs-FA and rCDs-pH show excellent lysosomal specificity (P = 0.89 and 0.83). By simply regulating the grafted functional response groups, this rCDs-based multifunctional sensing platform can be successfully fabricated for monitoring various biological targets.

0.3 g of citric acid, 100 μL of ethylenediamine, and 0.1 g of 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride were dispersed in 6 mL of ethanol, followed by ultrasonication for 10 min. Then the mixture was transferred to a 25-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained pre-product was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off of 500–1000 Da) for 24 h. Thereafter, the crude products were purified with silica column chromatography using the mixture of methanol and ethylacetate (1:5) as eluent. After drying under vacuum, the final product of rCDs was obtained.

20 mg of rCDs were first dispersed in 5 mL of ethanol. Subsequently, 200 μL of hydrazinium hydroxide was added and the mixture was refluxed at 80 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained product was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off of 500–1000 Da) for 24 h. After drying under vacuum, the final product of rCDs-FA was obtained.

10 mg of rCDs-FA were dispersed in 3 mL of ethanol. Subsequently, 200 μL of glyoxal was added and the mixture was refluxed at 80 °C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained product was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off of 500–1000 Da) for 24 h. After drying under vacuum, the final product of rCDs-HSO3 - was obtained.

20 mg of rCDs were dispersed in 3 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). Subsequently, 0.1 mg of CuSO4 and 500 μL of 4-(2-aminoethyl)-morpholine were added, and the mixture was heated at 125 °C for 16 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained product was diluted 20 times with water, and dialyzed with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off of 500–1000 Da) for 24 h. After drying under vacuum, the final product of rCDs-pH was obtained.

The stock solutions of CDs-FA and CDs-pH were prepared in 10 mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS) buffer, while the stock solution of CDs-HSO3 - was prepared in DMSO. To test the fluorescence responses, CDs-FA, CDs-pH and CDs-HSO3 - stock solutions were mixed with analytes, and diluted with PBS to 50 μg mL−1, 30 μg mL−1 and 80 μg mL−1, respectively. After vigorous shaking and standing for 1 h, the fluorescence spectra were recorded by an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 385 nm and excitation/emission slit width of 10 nm.

MCF-7 cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and appropriate amounts of antibiotics (penicillin and streptomycin) at 37 °C under 5% CO2. The cytotoxicity was studied by standard MTT assay. 2 × 104 cells mL−1 MCF-7 cells were seeded in 96-well plates and cultured for 12 h, and then incubated with various concentrations of rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - and rCDs-pH for 24 h, respectively. After that, 10 μL of 5 mg mL−1 MTT was added to each well, and incubated for 4 h. The supernatants were aspirated out, and 150 μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan. The absorbance at 570 nm was recorded, and cell viability (%) was determined by the following equation: Viability = (mean Abs. of treated wells/means Abs. of control wells) × 100%.

For imaging formaldehyde, MCF-7 cells were seeded in confocal dishes, incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 for 12 h, and then incubated with 200 µg mL−1 rCDs-FA for 4 h. After removing medium and washing three times with PBS, the cells were incubated with DMEM containing 0, 100 and 500 μM formaldehyde for 40 min, respectively. Because NaHSO3 can remove endogenous formaldehyde in cells, MCF-7 cells were treated by 400 μM NaHSO3 before staining with rCDs-FA as control group. After removing medium and washing three times with PBS, fluorescent images of cells were obtained by a FV-1200 confocal laser scanning microscope (λex = 405 nm, λem = 425–470 and 520–560 nm). The ratio was calculated using image-pro plus 6.0 software.

For imaging HSO3 -, MCF-7 cells were seeded in confocal dishes, incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 for 12 h, and then incubated with 100 µg mL−1 rCDs-HSO3 - for 4 h. After removing medium and washing three times with PBS, the cells were incubated with DMEM containing 1 mM NaHSO3 for 30 min as the experimental group. After washing three times by PBS, fluorescent images of cells were obtained by a FV-1200 confocal laser scanning microscope (λex = 405 nm, λem = 420–470 and 510–560 nm). The ratio was calculated using image-pro plus 6.0 software.

For imaging the lysosomal pH value, MCF-7 cells were seeded in confocal dishes, incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 for 12 h, and then incubated with 50 µg mL−1 rCDs-pH for 4 h. After removing medium and washing three times with PBS, the cells were incubated with DMEM containing 50 µM chloroquine for 30 min as the experimental group. After washing three times by PBS, fluorescent images of cells were obtained by a FV-1200 confocal laser scanning microscope (λex = 405 nm, λem = 420–470 and 510–560 nm). The ratio was calculated using image-pro plus 6.0 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and characterization of rCDs-based multifunctional sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare CDs with versatility and saclability for the construction of multifunctional ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform, citric acid and ethylenediamine, the common precursors to synthesize CDs with bright blue fluorescence [38–40], were selected to form the main structure of rCDs, which can generate polyamide network embedded small sp2 carbon nanodomains to provide stable blue emission as reference [41,42]. 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride, the common precursor of naphthalimide probes, combines with polyamide network to produce naphthalimide structure with bromine substitution site in rCDs. As a kind of common fluorophore, 4-bromo-naphthalimide has been widely used in the construction of fluorescent probes [43–45], which can be simply modified with various recognition or reaction groups at the 4th position of the naphthalimide ring to light up yellow-green fluorescent signal after responding to the specific target [46–53]. Benefiting from the maintained structure of naphthalimide structure with bromine substitution site to contribute responsive yellow-green fluorescence emission and the stable blue fluorescence emission from citric acid and ethylenediamine, dual emissive ratiometric sensors based on rCDs could be conveniently fabricated by avoiding the combination of two components with distinct emission wavelength together. As proof, three ratiometric fluorescent probes for formaldehyde, HSO3 - and pH were synthesized by introducing hydrazine, aldehyde and morpholine as response groups.

To verify the successful formation of carbon nanostructures, the morphology of rCDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1A, quasi-spherical rCDs were uniformly dispersed with an average diameter of 3.85 nm. After being functionalized with different response groups by the bromine substitution sites of rCDs, the structure of rCDs still remained well with only slight changes in average diameter, indicating that the functionalization of hydrazine, aldehyde and morpholine had no significant influence on the structure of rCDs (Fig. 1B–D). The average diameters of rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - and rCDs-pH were 3.73, 4.86 and 4.19 nm, respectively. Moreover, the growth and formation of rCDs were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S1, only irregular clumps could be observed when the synthesis time was 1 h and 2 h respectively, which might be attributed to the aggregation of polymers. When the synthesis time was increased to 4 h, small nanoparticles were presented, indicating the formation of rCDs. After 4 h, the particle size of rCDs increased gradually with the increase of synthesis time, suggesting the growth of rCDs. 6 h was selected as the final condition for the preparation of rCDs, because rCDs were already formed in this stage. Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out to investigate the chemical composition of rCDs, proving the successful functionalization of rCDs (Fig. 1E and F). As shown in the FT-IR spectra of rCDs (Fig. 1E), absorption peaks were found at about 2929.19 cm−1 and 1698.68 cm−1 in rCDs, which were assigned to groups of -CH3/-CH2 and CO, respectively. In high resolution XPS spectra of rCDs, strong C-C/CN peak in C1s spectrum and pyrrolic N peak in N1s spectrum were observed, and the peak of CO is significantly stronger than C-O in O1s spectrum (Fig. S2A-C). Taken together, the cross-linked polyamide structures widely existed in rCDs, demonstrating that the main structure of rCDs had been successfully constructed by citric acid and ethylenediamine. As shown in Fig. 1F, the peaks of Br 3d and Br 3p could be observed in XPS spectrum of rCDs, and Br element content of rCDs was calculated to be 1.51% (Table S1). The Br 3d spectrum revealed that Br element mainly existed in the form of C-Br (Fig. S2D), proving the successful introduction of naphthalimide structure with bromine substitution site in rCDs. After functionalization, Br contents in rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - and rCDs-pH dramatically reduced to 0.17%, 0.07% and 0.00% (Table S1), respectively, verifying that the modification of different response groups in rCDs is indeed achieved by the bromine substitution sites. The substitution rate could be calculated by the formula of (1 - Brfunctionalization/Broriginal) × 100%, and the substitution rates of rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - and rCDs-pH were 88.7%, 95.4%, and 100%, respectively. As shown in the FT-IR spectra in Fig. 1E, rCDs-FA showed stronger N-H stretching vibration peaks (3314.96 and 3191.97 cm−1) and C-N stretching vibration peak (1198.43 cm−1) than rCDs. The high resolution XPS spectra of rCDs-FA were similar to that of rCDs, whereas more amino N appeared in N1s spectrum (Fig. S3). The above results demonstrate that the successful functionalization of hydrazine in the bromine substitution sites of rCDs was realized to obtain rCDs-FA probe. Comparing with rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - have weaker N-H stretching vibration peak (Fig. 1E) and stronger C-N/CN peak (Fig. S4), verifying that the functionalization of aldehyde in rCDs-HSO3 - has been successfully implemented. Furthermore, the synthesis of rCDs-pH was also proved by the enhancement of CH3/CH2 stretching vibration peaks (2952.20, 2856.22 and 2816.76 cm−1) and C-O-C stretching vibration peak (1115.91 cm−1) in FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1E) and the increase of C-N/CN, pyrrolic N and C-O contents in high resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S5). In short, the chemical compositions of rCDs were highly accordance with our design assumptions, and modifications of rCDs had been successfully achieved to construct various probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: For studying the practicability of this multifunctional platform in ratiometric fluorescent sensing, fluorescent properties of rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - and rCDs-pH before and after reacting with their specific targets in PBS were first investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of blue emission gradually decreased while that of yellow-green emission gradually increased with the red shift of fluorescence excitation wavelength (370–405 nm). 385 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength for subsequent detection, because the relatively appropriate emission intensities of the two emission bands under excitation of 385 nm are prone to achieve the ratiometric fluorescent sensing. As the key to versatility and scalability of rCDs, the response performances of three probes to targets were systematically evaluated. Hydrazine quenches the fluorescence of naphthalamide structure by photoinduced electron transfer (PET), so pure rCDs-FA only show blue fluorescence. After reaction with formaldehyde, an extra signal of yellow-green fluorescence should appear due to the suppression of PET [39]. As expected, the yellow-green fluorescence intensity at 538 nm gradually enhanced with the increase of formaldehyde concentration from 0 to 40 μM, while the blue fluorescence intensity at 459 nm remained constant ( Fig. 2A). As shown in Fig. 2B, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I538/I459) presents a favorable linear relationship to formaldehyde concentration within 0–25 μM (y = 0.0368x + 0.227, R2 = 0.985). Similarly, rCDs-HSO3 - showed enhanced yellow-green fluorescence and stable blue fluorescence with the increase of HSO3 - concentration from 0 to 1 mM (Fig. 2 C), which is due to the non-radiative decay caused by CN isomerization restrained by intramolecular hydrogen bond between OH and CN after reaction with HSO3 - [37]. A favorable linear correlation between the logarithm of fluorescence intensity ratio (Log (I534/I459)) and the logarithm of HSO3 - concentration (Log [HSO3 -]) was successfully achieved (y = 0.0991x - 0.282, R2 = 0.998) in the HSO3 - concentration range of 20–800 μM (Fig. 2D). In neutral or alkaline environment, the fluorescence of naphthalamide structure in rCDs-pH is quenched by PET from the lone pair electrons of the N atom in morpholine to naphthalamide, whereas in acidic environment PET would be suppressed due to the protonation of N atom [42]. As shown in Fig. 2E, the intensities of two emission peaks of rCDs-pH both enhanced with the pH changes from 9.0 to 4.5. The value of I459/I524 was in a good linear relationship with pH in the range of 5.5–8.0 (y = 0.226x - 0.870, R2 = 0.977) (Fig. 2F). The above results prove that various probes derived from rCDs have satisfactory response to specific target, indicating the versatility and scalability of rCDs.

To further explore the practicality of rCDs as a versatile and scalable platform, the stability and selectivity of three probes were studied in detail. As shown in Fig. 3A and B, rCDs-FA and rCDs-HSO3 - show favorable pH stability within the physiological pH ranges, providing a solid foundation for their application in bio-sensing and bio-imaging. As shown in Fig. 3C–E, all the three probes display excellent selectivity to various potentially interfering species, including common anions, cations, amino acids, and target analogues. In addition, the photostability of three probes was evaluated by continuous irradiating of excitation source at 385 nm for 1 h. As shown in Fig. 3F, no significant attenuation was observed in both blue and yellow-green fluorescence of the three probes, suggesting the favorable photostability of the three probes. Subsequently, the effect of ionic strength for three probes was evaluated by comparing changes of their fluorescence intensity ratios in the absence and presence of high concentration NaCl. As shown in Fig. 4A, fluorescence intensity ratios of three probes before and after reacting with specific target were almost unaffected at NaCl concentration up to 2 M. Finally, the influences of probe concentrations on sensing performances were investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, concentrations of three probes ranging from 20 to 120 μg mL−1 did not significantly affect the fluorescence intensity ratios before and after the response, indicating the ability of three probes to correct interference of their own concentrations. Thus, probes based on rCDs own satisfactory stability and selectivity, endowing them with great potential in biological applications.

Then, response rate and reversibility of probes developed by this multifunctional sensing platform were investigated to further evaluate their sensing performances. Since rCDs-FA and rCDs-HSO3 - are reaction-based probes, it is important to study their kinetic profiles in the presence of analytes. After incubating rCDs-FA with 5 and 15 μM formaldehyde, both of fluorescence intensity ratio (I538/I459) of the systems reached maximum response at 27 min (Fig. 4B). As shown in Fig. S8, the rate constant of rCDs-FA is determined to be 0.11 min−1 under pseudo-first-order conditions. As for the response of rCDs-HSO3 - to HSO3 -, fluorescence intensity ration (I534/I459) reached equilibrium immediately within 30 s (Fig. 4C). The response of rCDs-pH to pH should be reversible, because this process is done by the protonation and deprotonation. Subsequently, the fluorescence reversibility of rCDs-pH against pH was studied by adjusting the pH value between 5.0 and 9.0 repeatedly. As shown in Fig. 4D, fluorescence intensity ratio (I524/I459) of rCDs-pH presented a good reproducibility between the acidic solution (pH = 5.0) and the alkaline solution (pH = 9.0) for 5 cycles. The above results indicate that the obtained rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - and rCDs-pH show satisfactory response rate and reversibility to their specific target, making them as potential excellent nanoprobes for intracellular measurement.

Encouraged by the results in vitro, intracellular suitability of this multifunctional sensing platform was further evaluated by choosing MCF-7 cells as a model. Prior to cellular imaging, MTT assay was conducted to investigate the cytotoxicity of rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - and rCDs-pH for MCF-7 cells. As shown in Fig. S9, rCDs-pH showed the best biocompatibility, and no significant drop of cell viability was observed at a high rCDs-pH concentration (up to 400 μg mL−1). When the concentration of rCDs-FA was below 300 μg mL−1, viability rate of MCF-7 cells was higher than 90%. Although rCDs-HSO3 - showed an obvious cytotoxicity at high concentrations, viability rate of MCF-7 cells was still above 90% at its working concentration (100 μg mL−1). The above results indicate that functionalized rCDs have favorable biocompatibility for applying in fluorescence imaging of living cells, and the toxicity of probes may be mainly due to the modified reactive groups such as -CHO.

Since morpholine is a common lysosomal targeting group, it is speculated that rCDs-pH may have lysosomal targeting ability. To verify this ability, colocalization experiment was implemented. After treating MCF-7 cells with rCDs-pH and commercial lysosome indicator Lyso-Traker DeepRed, the overlapped of green-yellow fluorescence from rCDs-pH and red fluorescence from Lyso-Traker DeepRed could be clearly observed. As shown in Fig. S10, Pearson’s colocalization coefficient of green-yellow and red channels in MCF-7 cells was calculated to be 0.83, demonstrating that rCDs-pH could be specifically distributed in lysosome. Interestingly, rCDs-FA also showed a lysosomal specific distribution (Fig. S11). The reason may be attributed to the protonation of -NH2 in the lysosome [54], which can be supported by the poor lysosomal specificity of rCDs-HSO3 - (Fig. S12).

The capability of the three probes for imaging of their specific targets in living MCF-7 cells was tested. As shown in Fig. 5A, the fluorescent intensity of MCF-7 cells incubated with rCDs-FA in blue channels almost kept constant in the presence of various concentrations of formaldehyde, while the emission intensity in yellow-green channels increased gradually when the concentration of formaldehyde changed from 0 to 500 μM. With increasing the concentrations of exogenous formaldehyde, the ratio value (yellow-green channel/blue channel) gradually increased. Moreover, HSO3 - as a formaldehyde scavenger was selected for the endogenous formaldehyde imaging. After pre-treating MCF-7 cells with HSO3 - (400 μM) for 40 min, the ratio value (yellow-green channel/blue channel) dramatically decreased when compared to the control group. The above results suggest that the designed rCDs-FA can be used to image both exogenous and endogenous formaldehyde in the lysosome of living MCF-7 cells. As shown in Fig. 5B, compared with control group, the fluorescence ratio value (yellow-green channel/blue channel) increased obviously after incubating with 1 mM HSO3 -, verifying its applicability in imaging intracellular HSO3 -. As reported, chloroquine is an anti-malarial drug that causes the increase of pH value in lysosomes [42]. MCF-7 cells were stimulated with chloroquine to observe the response of rCDs-pH to pH value change. As shown in Fig. 5C, after treatment with chloroquine, both of fluorescence intensities in blue and yellow-green channels significantly decreased. The fluorescence ratio of experimental group was significantly lower than that of control group, demonstrating that rCDs-pH can be used to detect pH value change of lysosome in living cells. Overall, three probes derived from rCDs have been successfully used to image targets in living MCF-7 cells, proving the practicability of this multifunctional sensing platform for construction of fluorescent probes in intracellular ratio imaging.

In conclusion, a novel strategy for construction of multifunctional sensing platform by grafting of various recognition or reaction groups on rCDs with easily modified bromine substitution site was rationally designed. Fascinatingly, rCDs not only provide stable blue emission as a reference due to polyamide network and the sp2 carbon nanodomains, but also present yellow-green emission responding to the targets after functionalization by virtue of naphthalimide structure, which facilitates the preparation of ratiometric fluorescent sensors to effectively reduce errors by self-correction. As proof, three ratiometric fluorescent probes for sensing formaldehyde (rCDs-FA), HSO3 - (rCDs-HSO3 -) and pH (rCDs-pH) were successfully constructed by introducing hydrazine, aldehyde and morpholine as response groups. By virtue of good stability, excellent selectivity and high biocompatibility, rCDs-FA, rCDs-HSO3 - and rCDs-pH could be applied to monitor the concentration variation of formaldehyde, HSO3 - and pH changes inside living cells, indicating their potential practical applications for the diagnosis of formaldehyde, HSO3 - and pH-related disease and disorder. This multifunctional sensing platform based on rCDs with easily modified active site can also provide a powerful tool to design other biosensors for the highly selective detection aims.

Guang-Yue Zou: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Lan Guo: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Shuai Chen: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Na-Zhen Liu: Writing – review & editing. Yong-Liang Yu: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922402, 21874017 and 21727811), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2005027). Special thanks are due to the instrumental analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shuai Chen received her PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry (2015) from Northeastern University (China). In 2013–2014, she worked with Professor Yong-min Liu as a visiting student at Northeastern University (USA). She is currently working as an Associate Professor in the Department of Chemistry, Northeastern University (China). Her current research focuses on the development of novel nanostructures for bioimaging imaging and biosensing techniques. She has published more than 30 papers in peer-reviewed international journals.
